title: General OPD Visit
description: Outpatient Department — for non-emergency illnesses, check-ups, and general consultations.
documents: Hospital folder / patient ID card (if you have visited before)
Referral letter (if referred from another facility)
Any previous test results or prescriptions
process: Go to the Records / Registration desk — arrive early (before 8am) for a lower queue number
Register and collect your folder. Pay a registration fee if not on NHIS or if NHIS is expired
Proceed to the Vitals room — nurses check your blood pressure, temperature, weight, and pulse
Wait in the OPD waiting area to be called by the doctor
Consultation with the doctor (5–15 minutes). The doctor may write a prescription or request tests
Go to the Laboratory or X-ray if tests are ordered
Return to doctor with results if required
Go to the Pharmacy to collect your medication
nhisCoverage: Consultation: ✅ Covered. Basic lab tests (malaria test, blood count): ✅ Covered. Some drugs: ✅ Covered. Specialist drugs and some premium medications: ⚠️ May require out-of-pocket payment.
tips: Arrive before 8:00am to get a low queue number
Bring a pen — many forms require your signature
Bring water and a small snack — waits can be long
Keep your folder and NHIS card together in a safe place for future visits
If you have a specific complaint, write it down beforehand so you don't forget in the consultation
cost: With NHIS: Usually GHS 0–5 for registration. Without NHIS: GHS 20–50+ depending on facility.

title: Antenatal Care (First Visit)
description: Your very first antenatal (pregnancy check-up) visit — usually around 8–12 weeks of pregnancy.
documents: Your husband's/partner's blood group results (if available)
process: Go to Records to register as an antenatal patient
You will be given an Antenatal (ANC) card — this is very important, keep it safe
Vitals are taken: weight, height, blood pressure, temperature
Blood and urine samples are collected for initial tests (HIV, blood group, haemoglobin, syphilis, malaria, protein in urine)
A midwife or doctor takes your comprehensive medical history
Physical examination including pelvic assessment
Doctor or midwife counsels you on nutrition, rest, danger signs in pregnancy, and supplements
Iron, folic acid, and other supplements are prescribed
Date for next visit is booked
nhisCoverage: All routine ANC services: ✅ Fully covered under NHIS. Deliveries: ✅ Covered. All ANC drugs (iron, folic acid, anti-malarials): ✅ Covered.
estimatedTime: 3–5 hours (first visit is the most thorough)
tips: Come with a URINE SAMPLE — collect first morning urine in a clean container with a lid
Fast from midnight if instructed (for blood sugar test) — ask when you call ahead
Bring a trusted person with you for support
Write down any questions or symptoms you want to discuss
Arrive early — ANC clinics fill up quickly
cost: With NHIS: GHS 0 for most services. Without NHIS: GHS 50–150 for full first visit.

title: Antenatal Care (Follow-up)
description: Routine follow-up antenatal visit after your first appointment.
documents: Your Antenatal (ANC) card — this is MANDATORY, do not forget it
Previous test results (if you had any done externally)
process: Go directly to ANC section — no need to start at Records if you have your ANC card
Weight and blood pressure are checked and recorded in your ANC card
Urine dipstick test may be done
Midwife listens to baby's heartbeat (after 20 weeks)
Fundal height measurement (belly measurement to assess baby's growth)
Any concerns or symptoms are reviewed
Refill of supplements (iron, folic acid) is given if needed
Next appointment date is recorded
nhisCoverage: All follow-up ANC visits: ✅ Covered. Supplements refill: ✅ Covered.
tips: DO NOT forget your ANC card — you may be turned away without it
Collect a urine sample before coming
Note any new symptoms to report: swelling, headaches, reduced fetal movement
ANC is ideally visited every 4 weeks up to 28 weeks, then every 2 weeks, then weekly near your due date
cost: With NHIS: GHS 0. Without NHIS: GHS 20–50 per visit.

title: Dental Check-up
description: Dental clinic visit for check-ups, extractions, fillings, or toothache treatment.
documents: Hospital folder (if you are a registered patient)
process: Register at Records and request Dental department
Take a seat in the Dental waiting area
Called in for examination — dentist examines your teeth and gums
Dentist discusses findings and recommends treatment
Treatment is carried out (extraction, cleaning, filling, etc.)
Prescription given if required
Follow-up appointment booked if needed
nhisCoverage: Basic examination: ✅ Covered. Tooth extraction: ✅ Covered. Fillings: ⚠️ Partially covered (basic fillings only). Cosmetic dental work: ❌ Not covered. X-rays: ⚠️ May require payment.
tips: Brush and floss before your appointment
Avoid eating 2 hours before if extractions are likely
Tell the dentist about any allergies to anaesthesia
Dental clinics often have separate queue systems — confirm the process at reception
cost: With NHIS: GHS 0–10 for basic services. Without NHIS: Extractions GHS 50–100, Fillings GHS 100–300.

title: Eye Clinic
description: Ophthalmology visit for eye problems, vision checks, or glasses prescription.
documents: Any previous eye prescriptions or glasses (bring them along)
process: Register at Records for the Eye Clinic department
Visual acuity test (reading chart test) is conducted by an optometrist
Intraocular pressure test may be done
Doctor examines the eyes with special instruments
Diagnosis and treatment plan discussed
Prescription for glasses or medication given
Referral to specialist given if needed
nhisCoverage: Eye examination: ✅ Covered. Basic eye medications: ✅ Covered. Cataract surgery (at participating hospitals): ✅ Covered. Glasses/lenses: ❌ Not covered by NHIS.
tips: Remove contact lenses at least 1–2 hours before your appointment if possible
Your eyes may be dilated during the appointment — bring sunglasses as your vision will be blurry for a few hours after
If bringing children for eye tests, plan for a longer visit and bring snacks to keep them calm
cost: With NHIS: GHS 0–20. Without NHIS: GHS 50–150 for full examination.

title: Laboratory Tests
description: Blood, urine, stool, and other diagnostic laboratory investigations.
documents: Doctor's request form (the lab form your doctor gave you — REQUIRED)
process: Present your laboratory request form at the Lab reception desk
Register and receive a sample collection number
Provide samples as required (blood draw, urine collection, stool sample, throat swab, etc.)
Wait for results — some tests are ready in 30–60 minutes, others take 24–48 hours
Collect printed results from the Lab
Return to your doctor with the results
nhisCoverage: Malaria test (RDT): ✅ Covered. Full Blood Count: ✅ Covered. HIV test: ✅ Covered. Liver function, kidney function: ✅ Covered at most facilities. Culture and sensitivity tests: ⚠️ Partially covered. Specialised genetic tests: ❌ Not covered.
estimatedTime: 30 minutes – 2 days depending on test type
tips: Some tests require FASTING — no food or water for 8–12 hours before. ASK your doctor if your test requires fasting
For urine tests: collect the MIDDLE of your urine stream (let a little out first) in a clean container
Bring the doctor's request form — you CANNOT get tested without it
Some tests have specific collection windows — ask when results will be ready before you leave
cost: With NHIS: GHS 0 for most basic tests. Without NHIS: GHS 20–200+ depending on test type.

title: X-Ray / Imaging
description: Radiological investigations including X-rays, ultrasounds, and CT scans.
documents: Previous imaging results to compare (if available)
process: Present your imaging request form at the Radiology/X-ray reception
Register and receive queue number
Change into hospital gown if required (for certain scans)
Procedure is carried out by a radiographer
Wait for images to be processed and interpreted by a radiologist (may take hours to days)
Collect your report and images — return to doctor with results
nhisCoverage: Plain X-ray (chest, limbs, spine): ✅ Covered. Ultrasound (obstetric and abdominal): ✅ Covered at most facilities. CT Scan: ⚠️ Covered at major hospitals but may have waiting lists. MRI: ❌ Usually not covered — very expensive out-of-pocket.
estimatedTime: 1–3 hours for the imaging; results may take longer
tips: Remove all metal objects — jewellery, belts, hair clips — before imaging
For abdominal ultrasound: drink 1 litre of water 1 hour before and do NOT urinate — a full bladder is needed
For obstetric ultrasound (pregnancy scans): same as above
Wear simple, easily removable clothing
Tell the radiographer if you are or may be pregnant — X-rays are avoided in early pregnancy
cost: With NHIS: GHS 0 for X-ray and basic ultrasound. Without NHIS: X-ray GHS 50–150, Ultrasound GHS 100–250, CT Scan GHS 500–1500.

title: Pharmacy Visit
description: Collecting prescribed medication from the hospital pharmacy.
documents: Doctor's prescription (REQUIRED — do not lose it)
process: Go to the outpatient pharmacy with your prescription
Submit prescription at the counter
Receive a queue number or wait for your name to be called
Your prescription is verified and dispensed
Pharmacist or dispensary technician explains dosage, timing, and storage
Pay any co-payment for non-covered items
nhisCoverage: Medicines on the NHIS Essential Medicines List: ✅ Covered. Brand-name and premium medications: ❌ May require full out-of-pocket payment. Some antiretrovirals and TB drugs: ✅ Free of charge.
tips: Do NOT lose the prescription — pharmacies won't dispense without it
Ask the pharmacist for clear instructions if you are unsure about dosage
If your drug is 'not available' on NHIS, ask for a generic/equivalent that is covered
Keep medications as instructed — many antibiotics must be refrigerated
Complete the full course of antibiotics even if you feel better
cost: With NHIS: GHS 0 for essential medicines. Non-covered drugs: GHS 10–500+ depending on medication.

title: Child Welfare Clinic
description: Growth monitoring, vaccinations, nutrition checks, and child health services.
documents: Child's Health Record Book ('Road to Health' book) — DO NOT FORGET THIS
Immunisation card (if separate from the health book)
process: Go to Child Welfare Clinic (CWC) section — usually a separate queue and area from OPD
Register child and present health record book
Child is weighed and measured (height/length)
Growth is plotted on the growth chart in the child's book
Vaccinations given if due
Nurse or doctor assesses nutritional status and development
Counselling on feeding, hygiene, and complementary foods given
Next appointment recorded in book
nhisCoverage: All routine childhood vaccinations: ✅ Free (EPI programme). Growth monitoring: ✅ Covered. Treatment for mild malnutrition: ✅ Covered. Specialised formula: ⚠️ May require payment.
tips: NEVER leave the Health Record book at home — all growth data and vaccine history is in it
CWC clinics often have specific days and hours (e.g., only on Tuesdays and Thursdays) — confirm beforehand
Dress the child in easily removable clothing for quick weighing
Bring an oral rehydration sachet in your bag in case the child reacts to vaccines
cost: With NHIS: GHS 0 for most services. Vaccinations are free for all children under the national programme.

title: Family Planning
description: Contraceptive counselling, birth control services, and reproductive health.
process: Go to Family Planning (FP) clinic — usually a separate section, often on specific days
Register at reception — this is a confidential service
Sit with a nurse or counsellor to discuss your options and medical history
Physical examination if required (blood pressure, weight)
Your chosen contraceptive method is administered (pills, injection, implant, IUD)
Counselling on correct use, potential side effects, and when to return
nhisCoverage: Contraceptive counselling: ✅ Covered. Injectable contraceptives (Depo-Provera): ✅ Covered. Implants: ✅ Covered. IUDs: ✅ Covered. Condoms: ✅ Usually free. Oral contraceptive pills: ✅ Covered.
tips: Family planning services are confidential — you do not need your partner's permission to access them
Know your last menstrual period (LMP) date — the nurse will ask
Most FP clinics run on specific days — call ahead to confirm
If you are starting a new method, ask clearly about when it becomes effective
cost: With NHIS: GHS 0 for most contraceptive services. The national programme subsidises most methods.

title: Emergency / Accident & Emergency (A&E)
description: For life-threatening emergencies — accidents, severe illness, trauma, chest pain.
documents: NHIS card (bring if you have it — but DO NOT delay emergency care to look for documents)
Any medications the patient is currently taking
Hospital folder (if available — not essential in emergency)
process: Go directly to the A&E / Casualty department — do not stop at Records
Tell the triage nurse the CHIEF complaint immediately and clearly
Triage assessment — the nurse categorises urgency (immediate, urgent, or can wait)
Doctor examination — may be rapid or detailed depending on severity
Investigations ordered (blood tests, X-ray, ECG, etc.)
Treatment initiated
Decision made: discharge, admit to ward, or transfer to specialist
nhisCoverage: Emergency services: ✅ Covered under NHIS. Note: if NHIS card is not available during an emergency, treatment should not be delayed — sort documentation afterwards.
estimatedTime: Variable — life-threatening cases are seen immediately. Less urgent cases may wait 2–6 hours.
tips: DO NOT delay going to the hospital for a real emergency to find documents
Tell the triage nurse the MOST serious symptom first — do not start from the beginning of the story
If you are driving someone in an emergency, call ahead so the hospital can prepare
Know the route to the nearest A&E before you need it
cost: Emergency treatment: generally covered by NHIS. Out-of-pocket: GHS 50–500+ depending on interventions.

title: Specialist Referral
description: Seeing a specialist physician (cardiologist, neurologist, surgeon, etc.) via referral.
documents: Referral letter from your OPD or primary doctor (REQUIRED)
All previous test results and imaging related to the condition
process: Specialists usually cannot be seen directly — you need a referral letter from OPD or your GP
Present referral letter at the Records desk and request the specialist department
Book an appointment or join the specialist clinic queue for that day
Wait to be seen — specialist clinics can have long waits (often 3–5 hours)
Specialist reviews your history, examinations, and previous results
May request further tests or procedures
Management plan discussed and documented
Follow-up appointment booked
nhisCoverage: Specialist consultation: ✅ Covered with valid referral letter. Specialist-ordered tests: ✅ Mostly covered. Some specialist medications: ⚠️ May require out-of-pocket payment.
estimatedTime: Half a day to a full day (specialist clinics are typically very busy)
tips: Get ALL your test results together and organised before the appointment
Bring a list of all your current medications with dosages
Specialist clinics often meet on specific days — confirm the schedule in advance
Bring a second person — the visit is long and information can be overwhelming
Write down questions you want answered before the appointment
cost: With NHIS: GHS 0 for basic specialist services. Some specialist investigations: GHS 50–500+.
